chunk_text:
The French Revolution began in 1789 and fundamentally transformed French society by ending the absolute monarchy and the feudal system. It was driven by financial crisis, food shortages, and growing resentment of the rigid class structure that divided France into three estates.

Causes of the Revolution

France's government had accumulated enormous debt from wars, including support for the American Revolution. King Louis XVI's attempts to reform taxation were blocked by the nobility, who refused to give up their exemptions. Meanwhile, poor harvests drove up bread prices, causing widespread hunger among the common people, known as the Third Estate.

The Reign of Terror

Between 1793 and 1794, the revolutionary government under Maximilien Robespierre executed thousands of perceived enemies of the revolution using the guillotine. This period, known as the Reign of Terror, was intended to suppress counter-revolutionary activity but ultimately led to Robespierre's own downfall and execution.

The French Revolution began in 1789 and fundamentally transformed French society by ending the absolute monarchy and the feudal system. It was driven by financial crisis, food shortages, and growing resentment of the rigid class structure that divided France into three estates.

Causes of the Revolution

France's government had accumulated enormous debt from wars, including support for the American Revolution. King Louis XVI's attempts to reform taxation were blocked by the nobility, who refused to give up their exemptions. Meanwhile, poor harvests drove up bread prices, causing widespread hunger among the common people, known as the Third Estate.

The Reign of Terror

Between 1793 and 1794, the revolutionary government under Maximilien Robespierre executed thousands of perceived enemies of the revolution using the guillotine. This period, known as the Reign of Terror, was intended to suppress counter-revolutionary activity but ultimately led to Robespierre's own downfall and execution.

The French Revolution began in 1789 and fundamentally transformed French society by ending the absolute monarchy and the feudal system. It was driven by financial crisis, food shortages, and growing resentment of the rigid class structure that divided France into three estates.

Causes of the Revolution

France's government had accumulated enormous debt from wars, including support for the American Revolution. King Louis XVI's attempts to reform taxation were blocked by the nobility, who refused to give up their exemptions. Meanwhile, poor harvests drove up bread prices, causing widespread hunger among the common people, known as the Third Estate.

The Reign of Terror

Between 1793 and 1794, the revolutionary government under Maximilien Robespierre executed thousands of perceived enemies of the revolution using the guillotine. This period, known as the Reign of Terror, was intended to suppress counter-revolutionary activity but ultimately led to Robespierre's own downfall and execution.

The French Revolution began in 1789 and fundamentally transformed French society by ending the absolute monarchy and the feudal system. It was driven by financial crisis, food shortages, and growing resentment of the rigid class structure that divided France into three estates.

Causes of the Revolution

France's government had accumulated enormous debt from wars, including support for the American Revolution. King Louis XVI's attempts to reform taxation were blocked by the nobility, who refused to give up their exemptions. Meanwhile, poor harvests drove up bread prices, causing widespread hunger among the common people, known as the Third Estate.

The Reign of Terror

Between 1793 and 1794, the revolutionary government under Maximilien Robespierre executed thousands of perceived enemies of the revolution using the guillotine. This period, known as the Reign of Terror, was intended to suppress counter-revolutionary activity but ultimately led to Robespierre's own downfall and execution.
